paper_title:
Spectral Insights into Data-Oblivious Critical Layers in Large Language
  Models
paper_abstract:
Understanding how feature representations evolve across layers in large
language models (LLMs) is key to improving their interpretability and
robustness. While recent studies have identified critical layers linked to
specific functions or behaviors, these efforts typically rely on data-dependent
analyses of fine-tuned models, limiting their use to post-hoc settings. In
contrast, we introduce a data-oblivious approach to identify intrinsic critical
layers in pre-fine-tuned LLMs by analyzing representation dynamics via Centered
Kernel Alignment(CKA). We show that layers with significant shifts in
representation space are also those most affected during fine-tuning--a pattern
that holds consistently across tasks for a given model. Our spectral analysis
further reveals that these shifts are driven by changes in the top principal
components, which encode semantic transitions from rationales to conclusions.
We further apply these findings to two practical scenarios: efficient domain
adaptation, where fine-tuning critical layers leads to greater loss reduction
compared to non-critical layers; and backdoor defense, where freezing them
reduces attack success rates by up to 40%.
related_work_a:
# Related Works

## Representation Similarity and Layer-wise Analysis

Understanding how internal representations evolve across the depth of neural networks has a long history that predates modern LLMs. Canonical correlation analysis was first adapted to deep learning as SVCCA [1], providing a tool for tracking learning dynamics and inter-layer similarity in deep networks. Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) was subsequently introduced as a more reliable similarity index that is invariant to orthogonal transformations and isotropic scaling, and that can identify meaningful correspondences between representations across different initializations [2]. CKA and related metrics have since become standard for probing hidden-state dynamics inside Transformer-based [3] language models, and our work builds directly on this tradition by using CKA to identify data-oblivious change points across layers.

## Interpreting Transformer Layers

A complementary line of work seeks to interpret what individual Transformer layers compute. Voita et al. analyze the bottom-up evolution of token representations under different training objectives, showing that layers encode progressively higher-level linguistic abstractions [4]. Geva et al. reveal that feed-forward blocks operate as key–value memories that store factual and lexical patterns [5], while Dar et al. project attention and FFN parameters directly into the embedding space to explain layer function [6]. The Tuned Lens refines the "logit lens" idea and traces how next-token predictions are progressively refined layer by layer [7]. Meng et al. localize factual associations to mid-layer feed-forward modules via causal tracing, motivating the ROME model-editing method [8]. Recent work also documents surprising phenomena such as massive activations concentrated in a small number of layers and tokens [9], and shows that intermediate layers—not the final ones—often yield the most useful representations for downstream tasks 10. Representation engineering pushes this perspective further, treating high-level concepts as directions in the hidden-state space that can be read out and manipulated for transparency and control [10]. In contrast to these largely data-dependent, post-hoc analyses, we identify change-point layers directly from a pre-fine-tuned model's spectral dynamics, independent of task data.

## Critical Layers and Efficient Fine-Tuning

A growing body of work argues that fine-tuning affects layers unevenly and that focusing updates on a subset of "critical" layers is both possible and desirable. LISA introduces layerwise importance sampling to make LLM fine-tuning memory-efficient by activating only a few layers per step 12. Gradient-based analyses further reveal that different layers are updated with markedly different magnitudes when training for "fast" versus "slow" reasoning, indicating that specific layers carry the dominant learning signal 13. Ren and Sutherland study the learning dynamics of LLM fine-tuning and characterize how updates propagate through the network, providing a theoretical grounding for layer-selective adaptation 14. All of these methods, however, require access to the fine-tuning data or gradients to determine which layers matter. Our data-oblivious criterion, derived purely from representation dynamics of the pre-fine-tuned model, complements this line of work by predicting the same critical layers before any task-specific data is observed.

## Safety, Backdoors, and Layer-Level Defense

Layer-wise analysis has also become central to LLM safety and adversarial robustness. Qi et al. show that even benign fine-tuning of aligned models can strip safety behavior 15, while gradient-based attacks such as GCG 16 can universally jailbreak aligned LLMs; red-teaming studies further map the harms these attacks can elicit 17. Recent defenses increasingly operate at the layer level: BEEAR removes backdoor triggers via embedding-space adversarial training 18, and layer-specific editing has been proposed as a defense against jailbreaks by surgically modifying only a small set of vulnerable layers 19. In the federated setting, Zhuang et al. show that poisoning a handful of "backdoor-critical" layers is sufficient to implant persistent triggers, underscoring how sensitive backdoor behavior is to a narrow subset of layers 20. Our findings echo and extend this trend: freezing the data-oblivious critical layers identified via CKA and spectral analysis substantially reduces backdoor attack success rates during fine-tuning, providing a principled, pre-training-only way to select which layers to protect.

paper_title:
Unlocking the Power of Rehearsal in Continual Learning: A Theoretical
  Perspective
paper_abstract:
Rehearsal-based methods have shown superior performance in addressing
catastrophic forgetting in continual learning (CL) by storing and training on a
subset of past data alongside new data in current task. While such a concurrent
rehearsal strategy is widely used, it remains unclear if this approach is
always optimal. Inspired by human learning, where sequentially revisiting tasks
helps mitigate forgetting, we explore whether sequential rehearsal can offer
greater benefits for CL compared to standard concurrent rehearsal. To address
this question, we conduct a theoretical analysis of rehearsal-based CL in
overparameterized linear models, comparing two strategies: 1) Concurrent
Rehearsal, where past and new data are trained together, and 2) Sequential
Rehearsal, where new data is trained first, followed by revisiting past data
sequentially. By explicitly characterizing forgetting and generalization error,
we show that sequential rehearsal performs better when tasks are less similar.
These insights further motivate a novel Hybrid Rehearsal method, which trains
similar tasks concurrently and revisits dissimilar tasks sequentially. We
characterize its forgetting and generalization performance, and our experiments
with deep neural networks further confirm that the hybrid approach outperforms
standard concurrent rehearsal. This work provides the first comprehensive
theoretical analysis of rehearsal-based CL.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

**Catastrophic forgetting and continual learning.** The tendency of neural networks to abruptly lose previously acquired knowledge when trained on new tasks — catastrophic forgetting — has been a central obstacle to continual learning (CL) since it was systematically documented in modern deep networks by 1. Subsequent surveys have organized the resulting literature into three broad families — regularization-based, architecture-based, and rehearsal-based approaches — and have benchmarked their stability–plasticity trade-offs 2, 3. Our work fits within this taxonomy but focuses specifically on when and how past data should be revisited.

**Regularization-based methods.** A first line of defense against forgetting adds penalties that discourage large changes to parameters deemed important for prior tasks. Elastic Weight Consolidation uses the Fisher information matrix to slow learning on task-critical weights 4, while Synaptic Intelligence accumulates an online path-integral estimate of per-synapse importance during training 5. These methods avoid storing data but are known to degrade when tasks are numerous or dissimilar, which motivates the memory-based approaches we build on.

**Rehearsal-based (replay) methods.** Rehearsal methods store a small subset of past examples and interleave them with current-task data during training. iCaRL couples exemplar herding with a distillation loss to learn class-incremental representations 6. Gradient Episodic Memory (GEM) projects the current gradient so it never increases loss on stored past samples, and introduces the standard average-accuracy, backward-transfer and forward-transfer metrics used throughout CL 7; Averaged-GEM (A-GEM) makes this constraint dramatically cheaper by using only the average memory gradient 8. CLEAR shows that in reinforcement learning a simple replay buffer combined with behavioral cloning already eliminates most forgetting 9. Dark Experience Replay (DER/DER++) additionally stores historical logits and matches them via distillation, providing one of the strongest general-CL baselines 10. Meta-Experience Replay (MER) reframes replay as a transfer-versus-interference trade-off, using Reptile-style meta-updates to explicitly align gradients between new and rehearsed examples 11. All of these methods share a *concurrent* rehearsal schedule: past and new samples are optimized together in the same batches. Our work questions whether this joint schedule is always optimal and studies *sequential* revisiting as an alternative.

**Theoretical analyses of continual and rehearsal-based learning.** A recent line of work analyzes CL in tractable models to isolate the roles of overparameterization, task similarity, and memory. In the Neural Tangent Kernel regime, closed-form expressions have been derived for orthogonal-gradient-descent variants, yielding the first generalization guarantees against catastrophic forgetting 12, and analogous NTK-based analyses have been used to guide parameter-efficient fine-tuning for CL 13. Overparameterized linear regression has proved especially fruitful: [1] shows that overparameterization alone can already mitigate forgetting under vanilla gradient descent, while 15 derives an exact closed-form for expected forgetting jointly as a function of task similarity and model width, uncovering non-monotone regimes where intermediate similarity is worst. Closest in spirit to our study, 16 analyzes multi-task and replay-based CL in overparameterized linear models, quantifying how buffer size, model capacity, and task similarity govern generalization and forgetting. Our analysis complements these works by being, to our knowledge, the first to theoretically compare *concurrent* and *sequential* rehearsal schedules in overparameterized linear models, to characterize the task-similarity regime in which each is preferred, and to derive from this comparison a hybrid rehearsal algorithm that we validate on deep networks.
related_work_b:
\textbf{Empirical studies in CL.}
CL has drawn significant attention in recent years, with numerous empirical approaches developed to mitigate the issue of catastrophic forgetting.
Architecture-based approaches combat catastrophic forgetting by dynamically adjusting network parameters \citep{rusu2016progressive} or introducing architectural adaptations such as an ensemble of experts \citep{rypescdivide}.
Regularization-based methods constrain model parameter updates to preserve the knowledge of previous tasks  \citep{kirkpatrick2017overcoming,magistrielastic}.
Memory-based methods address forgetting by storing information of old tasks in the memory and leveraging the information during current task learning, which can be further divided into orthogonal projection based methods and rehearsal-based methods. The former stores gradient information of old tasks to modify the optimization space for the current task \citep{saha2021gradient,lin2022trgp}, while the latter stores and reuses a tiny subset of representative data, known as exemplars. Exemplar sampling methods involve reservoir sampling \citep{cbrs2020} and an information-theoretic evaluation of exemplar candidates \citep{infors2022}. 
Other work such as \citet{shin2017continual} retains past knowledge by replaying "pseudo-rehearsal" constructed from input data instead of storing raw input.
Rehearsal methods mostly use a concurrent  scheme that trains the model using a mix of input data and sampled exemplars \citep{chaudhry2018efficient,chaudhry2019continual,rebuffi2017icarl,gargtic2024}.
Other exemplar utilization methods include \citet{lopez2017gradient} and \citet{chaudhry2018efficient}, which use exemplar to impose constraints in the gradient space.

\textbf{Theoretical studies in CL. } Compared to the vast amount of empirical studies in CL, the theoretical understanding of CL is very limited but has started to attract much attention very recently.
\citet{bennani2020generalisation,doan2021theoretical} investigated CL performance for the orthogonal gradient descent approach in NTK models theoretically. \citet{yin2020optimization} focused on regularization-based methods and proposed a framework, which requires second-order information to approximate loss function. \citet{cao2022provable,li2022provable} characterized the benefits of continual representation learning from a theoretical perspective. \citet{evron2023continual} connected regularization-based methods with Projection Onto Convex Sets. 
Recently, a series of theoretical studies proposed to leverage the tools of overparameterized linear models to facilitate better understanding of CL. \citet{evron2022catastrophic} studied the performance of forgetting under such a setup. After that, \citet{lin2023theory}  characterized the performance of CL, where they discuss the impact of task similarities and the task order. \citet{ding2024understanding} further characterized the impact of finite gradient descent steps on forgetting of CL. 
\citet{goldfarb2023analysis} illustrated the joint effect of task similarity and overparameterization.
\citet{zhao2024statistical} provided a statistical analysis of regularization-based methods.
More recently, \citet{li2024theory}  theoretically investigated the impact of mixture-of-experts on the performance of CL in linear models. 


Different from all these studies, we seek to fill up the theoretical understanding for rehearsal-based CL. Note that one concurrent study \citep{banayeeanzade2024theoretical} also investigates rehearsal-based CL in  linear models with concurrent rehearsal. However, one key difference here is that we propose a novel rehearsal strategy, i.e., the sequential rehearsal, and theoretically show its benefit over concurrent rehearsal for dissimilar tasks. Our theoretical results further motivate a new algorithm design for CL in practice, which demonstrates promising performance on DNNs.

paper_title:
Timing is important: Risk-aware Fund Allocation based on Time-Series
  Forecasting
paper_abstract:
Fund allocation has been an increasingly important problem in the financial
domain. In reality, we aim to allocate the funds to buy certain assets within a
certain future period. Naive solutions such as prediction-only or
Predict-then-Optimize approaches suffer from goal mismatch. Additionally, the
introduction of the SOTA time series forecasting model inevitably introduces
additional uncertainty in the predicted result. To solve both problems
mentioned above, we introduce a Risk-aware Time-Series Predict-and-Allocate
(RTS-PnO) framework, which holds no prior assumption on the forecasting models.
Such a framework contains three features: (i) end-to-end training with
objective alignment measurement, (ii) adaptive forecasting uncertainty
calibration, and (iii) agnostic towards forecasting models. The evaluation of
RTS-PnO is conducted over both online and offline experiments. For offline
experiments, eight datasets from three categories of financial applications are
used: Currency, Stock, and Cryptos. RTS-PnO consistently outperforms other
competitive baselines. The online experiment is conducted on the Cross-Border
Payment business at FiT, Tencent, and an 8.4\% decrease in regret is witnessed
when compared with the product-line approach. The code for the offline
experiment is available at https://github.com/fuyuanlyu/RTS-PnO.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

Our work sits at the intersection of three lines of research: (i) time-series forecasting for financial applications, (ii) end-to-end / decision-focused learning that couples prediction with a downstream optimization objective, and (iii) uncertainty quantification, in particular conformal calibration, for downstream risk control. We survey these areas below and situate the proposed Risk-aware Time-Series Predict-and-Allocate (RTS-PnO) framework within them.

**Time-series forecasting.** Modern deep forecasters underpin virtually all data-driven fund-allocation pipelines. Probabilistic recurrent models such as DeepAR [1] and interpretable basis-expansion architectures such as N-BEATS 2 established strong baselines for multi-horizon forecasting. A second wave of Transformer-based models pushed long-horizon accuracy further: Informer introduced ProbSparse attention for long sequences 3, Autoformer combined series decomposition with auto-correlation [2], and FEDformer exploited frequency-domain structure [3]. More recent designs continue to advance the state of the art, including patch-based tokenisation in PatchTST 6, variate-wise attention in iTransformer 7, and 2D temporal-variation modelling in TimesNet [4]. A parallel line of work has questioned whether Transformer complexity is necessary at all, showing that simple linear baselines can match or surpass these models on standard benchmarks 9. All of these forecasters remain purely prediction-oriented, and none of them account for how their forecasts will be consumed by a downstream allocation decision — a gap our framework explicitly addresses in a forecasting-model-agnostic manner.

**Predict-then-Optimize and decision-focused learning.** The naive pipeline of training a forecaster with a statistical loss and then plugging its point predictions into an optimizer is well known to induce a "goal mismatch" between the learning objective and the decision objective. The Smart "Predict, then Optimize" (SPO) framework 10 formalised this gap by defining the SPO loss directly on decision regret and deriving a convex SPO+ surrogate. In parallel, task-based end-to-end learning showed that differentiating through a stochastic program can yield forecasters better aligned with the downstream task, including in energy scheduling and inventory problems 11. OptNet made quadratic-program layers differentiable and thus embeddable within neural pipelines 12, and subsequent work extended decision-focused learning to combinatorial problems on graphs 13. A recent survey consolidates foundations, benchmarks, and open challenges of decision-focused learning 14. Portfolio-style allocation has also been tackled with deep reinforcement learning, e.g. the cryptocurrency portfolio manager of Jiang et al. 15; such approaches learn allocations directly but typically discard explicit forecasts and struggle to expose calibrated uncertainty. RTS-PnO instead retains a modular forecaster while performing end-to-end objective-aligned training around it, so any of the forecasters above can be plugged in without bespoke redesign.

**Uncertainty quantification and conformal calibration.** Because SOTA forecasters introduce non-trivial predictive uncertainty that can dominate allocation regret, calibrating this uncertainty is essential for risk-aware allocation. Conformal prediction offers distribution-free finite-sample coverage guarantees, and a comprehensive introduction is given by Angelopoulos and Bates [5]. Extensions to sequential and non-exchangeable data are particularly relevant to financial forecasting: EnbPI provides ensemble-based conformal intervals tailored to time series [6], and Adaptive Conformal Inference dynamically adjusts miscoverage under distribution shift [7]. RTS-PnO builds on this line by adaptively calibrating forecasting uncertainty and injecting it into the allocation objective, thereby unifying decision-focused training with distribution-free risk control and yielding consistent gains over predict-only, Predict-then-Optimize, and RL baselines across Currency, Stock, and Crypto benchmarks as well as a live Cross-Border Payment deployment.

paper_title: Aligning Protein Conformation Ensemble Generation with Physical Feedback
paper_abstract:
Protein dynamics play a crucial role in protein biological functions and
properties, and their traditional study typically relies on time-consuming
molecular dynamics (MD) simulations conducted in silico. Recent advances in
generative modeling, particularly denoising diffusion models, have enabled
efficient accurate protein structure prediction and conformation sampling by
learning distributions over crystallographic structures. However, effectively
integrating physical supervision into these data-driven approaches remains
challenging, as standard energy-based objectives often lead to intractable
optimization. In this paper, we introduce Energy-based Alignment (EBA), a
method that aligns generative models with feedback from physical models,
efficiently calibrating them to appropriately balance conformational states
based on their energy differences. Experimental results on the MD ensemble
benchmark demonstrate that EBA achieves state-of-the-art performance in
generating high-quality protein ensembles. By improving the physical
plausibility of generated structures, our approach enhances model predictions
and holds promise for applications in structural biology and drug discovery.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

**Diffusion and score-based generative models.** Denoising diffusion probabilistic models [1] and their continuous-time score-based counterparts 2, together with accelerated non-Markovian samplers such as DDIM [2], have become the dominant paradigm for generative modeling of complex distributions. Their ability to model rich, multi-modal densities in high-dimensional spaces makes them a natural fit for protein structure, where the target distribution is intrinsically continuous and geometrically constrained.

**Generative models for protein structure.** Building on this foundation, a series of SE(3)-equivariant diffusion and flow-matching models have been developed for generating physically plausible protein backbones. FoldingDiff 4 frames backbone generation as diffusion over torsion angles, EigenFold 5 performs harmonic diffusion for structure prediction, FrameDiff 6 introduces SE(3) diffusion over residue frames, Genie 7 diffuses oriented residue clouds, and FoldFlow 8 generalizes these ideas via stochastic flow matching on SE(3). While these methods primarily target de novo design and single-structure prediction, they establish the geometric backbones on which conformation samplers, including ours, are built.

**Protein conformation and ensemble generation.** Predicting a single crystallographic structure does not capture the dynamic ensembles that underlie protein function. Distributional Graphormer (DiG) 9 explicitly targets equilibrium distributions of molecular systems, Str2Str 10 repurposes structure predictors as zero-shot conformation samplers, and AlphaFlow/ESMFlow 11 re-cast AlphaFold and ESMFold as flow-matching generators to sample MD-like ensembles. ConfDiff 12 further introduces force guidance into SE(3) diffusion for conformation generation, while MDGen 13 generates entire molecular dynamics trajectories. These works form the closest benchmark set for our approach, and we compare against them on standard MD ensemble evaluations.

**Boltzmann generators and physics-aware learning.** A complementary line of work seeks to sample from the Boltzmann distribution directly. Boltzmann Generators 14 pioneered the use of normalizing flows trained with energy-based objectives; Timewarp 15 learns time-coarsened MD dynamics, coarse-grained diffusion has been coupled with force fields 16, and equivariant flow matching 17 together with transferable Boltzmann generators 18 push toward transferability across systems. High-accuracy machine-learned force fields such as NequIP 19 and MACE 20, reviewed extensively in 21, provide the physical signals that make energy-based supervision practical at scale. Our method integrates such physical feedback into a pretrained diffusion model without requiring differentiation through intractable energy landscapes.

**Aligning generative models with feedback.** Our approach is inspired by preference and reward-based fine-tuning developed for language and image models. RLHF-style optimization 22 and its offline variant Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) 23 reformulate alignment as a supervised classification problem over paired samples. These ideas have been adapted to diffusion models through reinforcement learning [3], policy-gradient fine-tuning 25, differentiable reward backpropagation such as AlignProp 26 and DRaFT 27, learned reward models like ImageReward 28, and preference-based Diffusion-DPO [4]. Our Energy-based Alignment (EBA) draws on this preference-optimization perspective but replaces human preferences with physical energy differences, yielding a tractable objective that calibrates protein conformation samplers against physics-based feedback.
related_work_b:
\paragraph{Protein conformation generation.}

Unlike structure prediction~\citep{jumper2021highly} aiming to identify a single, most-likely folded structure, protein conformation generation focuses on sampling an ensemble of physically plausible states that capture the underlying energy landscape.
Boltzmann generator~\citep{noe2019boltzmann} leverages normalizing flows to approximate the Boltzmann distribution by training on simulation data. 
\citet{arts2023two} applies the diffusion model to capture such distribution over coarse-grained protein conformations. 
EigenFold~\citep{jing2023eigenfold} adopts a generative perspective on structure prediction, enabling the generation of multiple structures given an input sequence. 
Str2Str~\citep{lu2024str2str} introduces a score-based sampler trained exclusively on PDB data, framing conformation generation in a structure-to-structure paradigm. 
DiG~\citep{zheng2024predicting} trains a conditional diffusion model on both PDB and in-house simulation data.
% allowing it to generate diverse conformations. 
ConfDiff~\citep{wang2024proteinconformationgenerationforceguided} incorporates the energy- and force-guidance during the reverse process of diffusion to enhance the accuracy of conformation generation.
AlphaFlow~\citep{jing2024alphafoldmeetsflowmatching} repurposes the AlphaFold2 model into a denoising network via flow matching. 
ESMDiff~\citep{lu2024structure} fine-tunes the protein language model ESM3 using discrete diffusion to produce protein conformations. 
Finally, MDGen~\citep{jing2024generative} attempts direct generation of MD trajectories by modeling them as time-series of protein structures.

\vspace{-4pt}
\paragraph{Alignment methods for generative models.}

Aligning generative models with desired objectives is becoming increasingly important. The Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) framework optimizes models via RL using human preference rewards and has been widely applied in tasks like machine translation~\citep{kreutzer2018reliability}, summarization~\citep{stiennon2020learning}, and instruction following~\citep{ouyang2022training}. RLHF has also been applied for alignment of text-to-image diffusion models~\citep{black2023training, fan2024reinforcement}. However, RL-based fine-tuning faces significant challenges in stability and scalability.
Direct Preference Optimization ~\citep{rafailov2024direct} mitigates these issues by directly optimizing for the optimal policy via re-parameterization of an implicit reward model.  This approach has been extended beyond language modeling: Diffusion-DPO \cite{Wallace_2024_CVPR} for text-to-image generation, ABDPO \cite{zhou2024antigen} for antibody design using Rosetta energy \cite{alford2017rosetta}, and ALIDIFF \cite{gu2024aligning} and DECOMPDPO \cite{cheng2024decomposed} for molecular optimization in structure-based drug design.

\textit{Remarks: Our method differs from existing approaches above by adopting a more general-form objective, being grounded in physically meaningful motivations, addressing a different task and demonstrating superior performance.}

paper_title:
CLONE: Customizing LLMs for Efficient Latency-Aware Inference at the
  Edge
paper_abstract:
Deploying large language models (LLMs) on edge devices is crucial for
delivering fast responses and ensuring data privacy. However, the limited
storage, weight, and power of edge devices make it difficult to deploy
LLM-powered applications. These devices must balance latency requirements with
energy consumption and model accuracy. In this paper, we first quantify the
challenges of deploying LLMs on off-the-shelf edge devices and then we present
CLONE, an in-depth algorithm-hardware co-design at both the model- and
system-level that intelligently integrates real-time, energy optimization while
maintaining robust generality. In order to maximize the synergistic benefits of
these algorithms in always-on and intermediate edge computing settings, we
specialize in a 28nm scalable hardware accelerator system. We implement and
extensively evaluate CLONE on two off-the-shelf edge platforms. Experiments
show that CLONE effectively accelerates the inference process up to 11.92x, and
saves energy up to 7.36x, while maintaining high-generation.
related_work_a:
# Related Works

**Large Language Models and the Edge Deployment Challenge.** The rapid scaling of transformer-based large language models (LLMs), such as GPT-3 [1], LLaMA [2], and LLaMA 2 [3], has produced unprecedented natural language capabilities at the cost of massive compute, memory, and energy requirements. Broad surveys of efficient LLM techniques 4 and of resource-efficient foundation-model systems [4] systematically catalog the algorithmic and system-level barriers that make deploying such models on storage-, power-, and thermally-constrained edge devices exceptionally difficult. CLONE targets exactly this deployment gap through a co-design that couples model compression with a specialized 28 nm accelerator.

**Weight and Activation Quantization.** A dominant line of prior work reduces LLM footprint through low-precision numerics. LLM.int8() shows that emergent outlier features can be handled by mixed-precision decomposition to preserve accuracy at 8 bits [5]. GPTQ generalizes second-order post-training quantization down to 3–4 bits for billion-parameter models [6], while SmoothQuant migrates activation outliers into weights to enable joint W8A8 quantization [7]. AWQ [8] and SqueezeLLM [9] further push into 4-bit and non-uniform, dense-and-sparse regimes tailored to salient weights. GOBO exemplifies how quantization can be exploited by a dedicated NLP accelerator for latency and energy savings 11. CLONE builds on this literature but jointly optimizes quantization with hardware datapaths on always-on edge platforms rather than server GPUs.

**Pruning and Sparsity.** Complementing quantization, structured and unstructured pruning shrink model size and compute. SparseGPT enables one-shot 50%+ pruning of massive LLMs with negligible perplexity loss 12, and Wanda proposes a simple activation-aware magnitude criterion that requires no retraining 13. Beyond static pruning, contextual sparsity approaches such as Deja Vu dynamically skip attention heads and MLP neurons at inference time 14, while H2O maintains only a small heavy-hitter subset of the KV cache 15. These dynamic sparsity patterns motivate CLONE's real-time energy-optimization pathway, but they are typically evaluated on GPU systems and rarely address the memory-hierarchy realities of edge SoCs.

**Efficient Attention and Serving Systems.** Attention is a primary latency and energy bottleneck for LLM inference. FlashAttention reorganizes the attention kernel to be IO-aware, avoiding materialization of the full attention matrix 16. PagedAttention/vLLM introduces virtual-memory-style KV-cache management for high-throughput serving [10], and StreamingLLM enables unbounded-length generation by preserving attention-sink tokens 18. Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by verifying draft-model tokens in parallel [11]. These techniques inform, but do not solve, the edge constraints CLONE addresses.

**On-Device and Mobile LLM Inference.** A growing body of work targets constrained hardware directly. LLM in a Flash streams parameters from flash memory using sparsity- and locality-aware loading 20. PowerInfer exploits neuron-activation locality to combine a consumer GPU with CPU offloading [12], and EdgeMoE partitions mixture-of-experts LLMs across the mobile memory hierarchy 22. LLMCad co-schedules a small on-device generator with a large verifier for fast mobile inference 23, while MobileLLM redesigns sub-billion parameter architectures specifically for on-device use 24. These works stay largely at the software or commodity-hardware layer.

**Hardware Accelerators for Transformer Inference.** Finally, custom accelerators explicitly co-designed with attention algorithms provide a template for CLONE's silicon. A^3 approximates attention scoring to accelerate the dot-product bottleneck 25, and SpAtten introduces cascade token and head pruning tightly coupled to a dedicated architecture 26. CLONE extends this hardware-algorithm co-design paradigm to a 28 nm scalable edge accelerator that unifies quantization, sparsity, and dynamic energy optimization while preserving generality across always-on and intermediate edge workloads.

paper_abstract:
Vision Transformers (ViTs) leverage the transformer architecture to
effectively capture global context, demonstrating strong performance in
computer vision tasks. A major challenge in ViT hardware acceleration is that
the model family contains complex arithmetic operations that are sensitive to
model accuracy, such as the Softmax and LayerNorm operations, which cannot be
mapped onto efficient hardware with low precision. Existing methods only
exploit parallelism in the matrix multiplication operations of the model on
hardware and keep these complex operations on the CPU. This results in
suboptimal performance due to the communication overhead between the CPU and
accelerator. Can new data formats solve this problem?
  In this work, we present the first ViT accelerator that maps all operations
of the ViT models onto FPGAs. We exploit a new arithmetic format named
Microscaling Integer (MXInt) for datapath designs and evaluate how different
design choices can be made to trade off accuracy, hardware performance, and
hardware utilization. Our contributions are twofold. First, we quantize ViTs
using the MXInt format, achieving both high area efficiency and accuracy.
Second, we propose MXInt-specific hardware optimization that map these complex
arithmetic operations into custom hardware. Within 1\% accuracy loss, our
method achieves at least 93$\times$ speedup compared to Float16 and at least
1.9$\times$ speedup compared to related work.
related_work_a:
# Related Work

## Vision Transformers

The Transformer architecture [1] was adapted to vision by the Vision Transformer (ViT), which shows that a pure self-attention model applied to sequences of image patches can match or outperform convolutional networks on large-scale image classification [2]. Follow-up work reduces the data requirements through distillation 3 and introduces hierarchical designs with shifted-window attention for general-purpose backbones 4. Our work targets the efficient hardware execution of exactly this family of models, whose compute is dominated by dense matrix multiplication but whose accuracy depends on nonlinear operations such as Softmax, GELU, and LayerNorm.

## Quantization of Transformers and Vision Transformers

A large body of work has explored low-precision integer quantization for Transformer-based models. For language models, Q8BERT 5 and I-BERT 6 demonstrate 8-bit and integer-only inference, while more recent post-training methods address the accuracy cliffs of large models: LLM.int8() isolates outlier channels [3], SmoothQuant migrates the quantization difficulty between activations and weights [4], and GPTQ performs one-shot weight quantization via approximate second-order information [5]. For ViTs specifically, Liu et al. propose a similarity-aware post-training scheme 10, PTQ4ViT introduces twin uniform quantization tailored to post-Softmax and post-GELU activations 11, FQ-ViT quantizes LayerNorm and Softmax with power-of-two factors and log-int softmax 12, Q-ViT enables fully differentiable quantization search 13, and I-ViT provides integer-only inference by replacing nonlinear functions with bit-shift approximations 14. These schemes highlight that plain per-tensor INT8 is insufficient for ViT nonlinearities, motivating richer numerical formats such as the one we adopt.

## Block-scaled and Microscaling Data Formats

Recent formats trade a single per-tensor scale for finer-grained shared scales. FP8 has been standardized for deep learning training and inference 15. Block floating-point variants such as FlexBlock support multi-mode training accelerators 16, and theoretical analyses characterize the effect of block size on quantization error 17. Shared microexponent formats extend this idea with hierarchical, small exponent groups to close the accuracy gap to FP32 at very low bit widths 18, and the open Microscaling (MX) specification unifies these designs into standard MXInt/MXFP formats for cross-vendor deployment 19. Our accelerator builds on MXInt but is the first to co-design its arithmetic pipeline for all ViT operations, including nonlinearities, on FPGAs.

## FPGA and ASIC Accelerators for Vision Transformers

Several accelerators target ViT inference on programmable hardware. VAQF is a software-hardware co-design framework that maps low-bit ViTs to FPGAs with per-layer precision selection 20. Auto-ViT-Acc explores mixed-scheme (fixed-point and power-of-two) quantization with an FPGA-aware search 21. HeatViT combines hardware-efficient adaptive token pruning with 8-bit quantization 22. ViTCoD prunes and polarizes the attention map to expose regular sparsity patterns for a dedicated accelerator 23, and ViTALiTy unifies low-rank and sparse approximations into a linear-Taylor attention accelerator 24. A key limitation shared by these works is that non-GEMM operations (Softmax, LayerNorm) are either kept at high precision or offloaded to a host CPU, introducing communication overhead. In contrast, we map every operation of the ViT onto the FPGA using MXInt datapaths.

## Hardware Optimization of Softmax and Attention

Because Softmax dominates the non-GEMM cost of Transformers, dedicated arithmetic units have been proposed. Softermax replaces the base-e exponential with a base-2 online-normalized approximation for hardware efficiency 25. A$^3$ accelerates attention through candidate approximation and pruning 26, and gradient-based learned runtime pruning skips low-magnitude Softmax outputs during inference 27. Hyft designs a reconfigurable Softmax unit with a hybrid numeric format for training and inference 28. Our design differs by leveraging the shared-exponent structure of MXInt to fold the exponent bias directly into the Softmax and LayerNorm pipelines, enabling large speedups over Float16 and prior FPGA-based ViT accelerators within a 1% accuracy budget.

paper_title:
NMP-PaK: Near-Memory Processing Acceleration of Scalable De Novo Genome
  Assembly
paper_abstract:
De novo assembly enables investigations of unknown genomes, paving the way
for personalized medicine and disease management. However, it faces immense
computational challenges arising from the excessive data volumes and
algorithmic complexity.
  While state-of-the-art de novo assemblers utilize distributed systems for
extreme-scale genome assembly, they demand substantial computational and memory
resources. They also fail to address the inherent challenges of de novo
assembly, including a large memory footprint, memory-bound behavior, and
irregular data patterns stemming from complex, interdependent data structures.
Given these challenges, de novo assembly merits a custom hardware solution,
though existing approaches have not fully addressed the limitations.
  We propose NMP-PaK, a hardware-software co-design that accelerates scalable
de novo genome assembly through near-memory processing (NMP). Our channel-level
NMP architecture addresses memory bottlenecks while providing sufficient
scratchpad space for processing elements. Customized processing elements
maximize parallelism while efficiently handling large data structures that are
both dynamic and interdependent. Software optimizations include customized
batch processing to reduce the memory footprint and hybrid CPU-NMP processing
to address hardware underutilization caused by irregular data patterns.
  NMP-PaK conducts the same genome assembly while incurring a 14X smaller
memory footprint compared to the state-of-the-art de novo assembly. Moreover,
NMP-PaK delivers a 16X performance improvement over the CPU baseline, with a
2.4X reduction in memory operations. Consequently, NMP-PaK achieves 8.3X
greater throughput than state-of-the-art de novo assembly under the same
resource constraints, showcasing its superior computational efficiency.
related_work_a:
## Related Work

### Distributed and Large-Scale de novo Genome Assembly

Early work on memory-efficient de Bruijn graph construction explored data structures that shrink the k‑mer representation to make de novo assembly feasible on commodity servers. SparseAssembler introduced a sparse de Bruijn graph that skips intermediate k‑mers to reduce memory from O((2k+8)N) to approximately O((2k/g+8)N), also incorporating graph-based denoising to tolerate sequencing errors [1](https://arxiv.org/abs/1106.2603). While this significantly cuts memory, it still assumes conventional CPU-centric execution and does not fundamentally address memory bandwidth limits or irregular data access at scale.

To handle population-scale datasets, several assemblers were redesigned for massive distributed-memory machines. HipMer parallelizes the Meraculous pipeline end‑to‑end for extreme-scale whole-genome assembly, carefully engineering distributed hash tables and communication patterns to assemble human and large plant genomes on tens of thousands of cores [2](https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.11147). MetaHipMer extends this approach to de novo metagenome assembly, combining an iterative de Bruijn graph strategy with specialized scaffolding for complex microbiomes, and demonstrating strong scaling on multi-terabyte environmental datasets [3](https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.07014). These systems highlight that the dominant costs arise from memory-bound k‑mer counting, graph construction, and traversal over massive, irregular data structures. However, they remain built on traditional CPU clusters, so their performance and memory footprint are fundamentally constrained by off-chip DRAM bandwidth and capacity.

### Near-Data and In-Storage Acceleration for Genome Analysis

Recognizing that data movement dominates energy and time costs, recent work has explored pushing computation closer to where genomic data resides. GenStore proposes an in-storage computing system that embeds lightweight accelerators and filters inside NAND flash SSDs to offload large portions of the read-mapping pipeline [4](https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.10400). By co-designing SSD-resident filters and data layouts with the host software, GenStore reduces both data transfer and compute overheads, achieving multi-fold speedups and energy savings. While architecturally distant from near-memory DRAM, GenStore exemplifies how hardware/software co-design around storage hierarchies can shrink memory traffic for genome analysis.

Complementary to in-storage computing, several works leverage processing-in-memory (PIM) or near-memory compute for seed filtering and alignment. GRIM‑Filter introduces a seed-location filter tailored to 3D‑stacked memories with logic layers, using massively parallel in-memory bitwise operations to eliminate most candidate locations before alignment [5](https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.01177). RAPIDx targets dynamic-programming-based sequence alignment with a ReRAM PIM architecture and an adaptive banded algorithm co-designed to fit the PIM substrate, yielding large throughput and energy gains over CPU, GPU, and earlier PIM designs [6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.05733). DART‑PIM presents a digital PIM framework for end-to-end DNA read mapping, reorganizing indexing, filtering, and alignment around the constraints and opportunities of PIM arrays [7](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.03832). These accelerators demonstrate the value of exploiting high internal memory bandwidth, but focus on read mapping or alignment kernels rather than full de novo assembly pipelines, and they typically operate over relatively regular dynamic-programming or bitvector computations.

### Toward Hardware/Software Co-design for Assembly

The above PIM and in-storage systems primarily target read mapping stages where access patterns, although large, are more structured. In contrast, de novo assembly requires managing massive, sparse, and dynamically evolving graph structures, which amplify issues of load imbalance, pointer chasing, and memory capacity pressure. Existing distributed assemblers such as HipMer and MetaHipMer already expose considerable parallelism but ultimately rely on general-purpose CPUs and host memory [2](https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.11147)[3](https://arxiv.org/abs/1809.07014). At the same time, low‑level de Bruijn data-structure innovations like SparseAssembler optimize memory usage but do not exploit specialized hardware capabilities [1](https://arxiv.org/abs/1106.2603).

NMP‑PaK differs from these prior efforts by explicitly co-designing a channel-level near-memory processing architecture and the de novo assembly software pipeline. Instead of accelerating only seed filters or alignment subroutines, it targets the scalability bottlenecks of graph-centric assembly itself: the construction and manipulation of large, interdependent data structures under highly irregular access patterns. Unlike GenStore’s flash-centric design [4](https://arxiv.org/abs/2202.10400) or PIM accelerators specialized for alignment [5](https://arxiv.org/abs/1711.01177)[6](https://arxiv.org/abs/2211.05733)[7](https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.03832), NMP‑PaK restructures de novo assembly around channel-local scratchpads and custom processing elements, and further introduces batch scheduling and hybrid CPU–NMP execution to mitigate hardware underutilization. In doing so, it bridges the gap between distributed CPU-only assemblers and fine-grained PIM accelerators, providing a holistic near-memory solution for end-to-end, large-scale de novo genome assembly.

%satish and mutlur's works



\paragraph{\textbf{In/Near-memory processing accelerators for \textit{de novo} assembly}}

Previous work has explored accelerating De Bruijn graph-based genome assembly using in/near-memory processing. 
Zhou et al. \cite{zhou2021ultra} enhanced the Megahit assembly algorithm \cite{li2015megahit} through the use of hybrid memory cubes, exploiting high degrees of parallelism and enhanced memory bandwidth across near-data processing cores. 
However, the Megahit algorithm does not scale well with increasing genome sample data sizes, and hybrid memory cubes have been discontinued since 2018.

Angizi et al. \cite{angizi2020pim} introduced PIM-Assembler, a processing-in-DRAM platform for \textit{de novo} assembly.
Their design achieves high performance and energy efficiency through in-DRAM X(N)OR operations for accelerating comparisons and additions, along with optimized data partitioning and mapping techniques.
However, while effective for smaller genomes (9.2 GB), their approach has not been demonstrated for extremely large-scale \textit{de novo} assembly tasks that we address in our work.

While our work focuses on accelerating the Iterative Compaction step, Wu et al. \cite{wu2024abakus} accelerated the k-mer counting step using in-storage processing. 
Their method leverages the embedded CPU controllers and DRAM within the solid-state drive, thereby reducing data transfer between the SSD and the main memory.
In contrast, \NMP\ aims to speed up the Iterative Compaction step, which is the most time-consuming phase.

\paragraph{\textbf{GPU-based \textit{de novo} assembly accelerators}}

Several works \cite{awan2021accelerating,goswami2018gpu,mahmood2011gpu,jain2013gagm,swiercz2018grasshopper} leveraged GPUs' parallel processing capabilities and high bandwidth to accelerate specific assembly operations, such as local assembly, sorting, and prefix scan.

However, GPUs face significant limitations for large-scale genome assembly. 
Their restricted onboard memory capacity \cite{zhou2021ultra} makes them inadequate for extremely large datasets. 
Additionally, genome assembly algorithms present challenges for GPUs due to their memory-intensive nature and irregular access patterns during graph construction and traversal \cite{awan2021accelerating}.

%GAGM \cite{jain2013gagm} DBG offload most of the assembly to GPU
%GrassHopper \cite{swiercz2018grasshopper} sequence alignment to GPUs



\paragraph{\textbf{Non-de Bruijn graph assembly accelerators}}

%%% For minimap2 and bwa-mem2
There are other algorithms that researchers use when new genomes are discovered.
These algorithms uncover new genomes through sequence mapping and alignment with reference genomes.
%Unlike the DBG method, which assembles genomes from scratch, 
%These algorithms uncover new genomes by comparing them with existing reference genomes. 
BWA-MEM \cite{bwa-mem2} and \texttt{minimap2} \cite{minimap2} are widely used solutions for short-read and long-read alignment, respectively. 
%These tools perform sequence mapping and alignment of newly discovered genomes with existing ones.

%and are crucial for identifying and classifying different SARS-CoV-2 strains, as well as for taxonomic classification based genomic similarities.

Multiple studies have explored accelerating these algorithms.
%BWA-MEM and \texttt{minimap2} to improve their performance.
\texttt{mm2-fast} \cite{mm2-fast} accelerated the \texttt{minimap2} algorithm on CPUs using SIMD optimizations, while \texttt{mm2-ax} \cite{mm2-ax} took a cooperative approach by designing a heterogeneous system involving both the CPU and GPU. % mapping computational steps to the architecture that best accelerates them.
\texttt{mm2-gb} \cite{mm2-gb} further improved \texttt{mm2-ax} for long-read sequence mapping by running all the steps on the GPU.
Additionally, Guo et al. \cite{guo2019hardware} proposed a custom accelerator for sequence mapping using FPGA.
Beyond sequence-to-sequence mapping algorithms like \texttt{minimap2}, several accelerators targeted sequence-to-graph mapping of long reads, including SeGraM \cite{cali2022segram} and Harp \cite{zhang2024harp}.

Unlike the above works, \NMP\ addresses genome assembly without the need for a reference genome. 
In addition to performance gains, \NMP\ focuses on reducing the memory footprint and resource requirements for large datasets.


\paragraph{\textbf{Accelerators for other genomic applications}}

MegIS \cite{ghiasi2024megis} addressed the significant data movement overhead in metagenomics analysis through in-storage processing. %reducing the transfer of large volumes of low-reuse data across the memory hierarchy.
While GenDP \cite{gu2023gendp} and QUETZAL \cite{pavon2024quetzal} accelerated dynamic programming algorithms (Smith-Waterman, Needleman-Wunsch) that are essential kernels in various genomic applications, including reference-guided assembly, \textit{de novo} assembly, metagenomics, and sequence analysis for both long and short reads, our work addresses similar fundamental challenges of processing large, interdependent, and dynamic data structures.
However, we focus on end-to-end \textit{de novo} assembly performance, prioritizing efficient computation with minimal hardware resources while handling large memory requirements.

paper_title:
Efficient Serverless Cold Start: Reducing Library Loading Overhead by
  Profile-guided Optimization
paper_abstract:
Serverless computing abstracts away server management, enabling automatic
scaling, efficient resource utilization, and cost-effective pricing models.
However, despite these advantages, it faces the significant challenge of
cold-start latency, adversely impacting end-to-end performance. Our study shows
that many serverless functions initialize libraries that are rarely or never
used under typical workloads, thus introducing unnecessary overhead. Although
existing static analysis techniques can identify unreachable libraries, they
fail to address workload-dependent inefficiencies, resulting in limited
performance improvements. To overcome these limitations, we present SLIMSTART,
a profile-guided optimization tool designed to identify and mitigate
inefficient library usage patterns in serverless applications. By leveraging
statistical sampling and call-path profiling, SLIMSTART collects runtime
library usage data, generates detailed optimization reports, and applies
automated code transformations to reduce cold-start overhead. Furthermore,
SLIMSTART integrates seamlessly into CI/CD pipelines, enabling adaptive
monitoring and continuous optimizations tailored to evolving workloads. Through
extensive evaluation across three benchmark suites and four real-world
serverless applications, SLIMSTART achieves up to a 2.30X speedup in
initialization latency, a 2.26X improvement in end-to-end latency, and a 1.51X
reduction in memory usage, demonstrating its effectiveness in addressing
cold-start inefficiencies and optimizing resource utilization.

paper_title:
Kubernetes in the Cloud vs. Bare Metal: A Comparative Study of Network
  Costs
paper_abstract:
Modern cloud-native applications increasingly utilise managed cloud services
and containerisation technologies, such as Kubernetes, to achieve rapid
time-to-market and scalable deployments. Organisations must consider various
factors, including cost implications when deciding on a hosting platform for
containerised applications as the usage grows. An emerging discipline called
FinOps combines financial management and cloud operations to optimise costs in
cloud-based applications. While prior research has explored system-level
optimisation strategies for cost and resource efficiency in containerized
systems, analysing network costs in Kubernetes clusters remains underexplored.
This paper investigates the network usage and cost implications of
containerised applications running on Kubernetes clusters. Using a methodology
that combines measurement analysis, experimentation, and cost modelling, we aim
to provide organisations with actionable insights into network cost
optimisation. Our findings highlight key considerations for analysing network
expenditures and evaluating the potential cost benefits of deploying
applications on cloud providers. Overall, this paper contributes to the
emerging FinOps discipline by addressing the financial and operational aspects
of managing network costs in cloud-native environments.
